description: This figure includes two schematic diagrams of test apparatuses for investigating the cyclic behavior of Fe-Mn-Al-C austenitic steels. Subfigure (a) shows the thermocycling test apparatus: it fixes a φ5 mm specimen on restriction supports with 80% thermal expansion restriction, is equipped with a thermocouple for temperature monitoring, and connects to an AC power supply and automatic temperature controlled variable resistor to achieve cyclic temperature alternation between 313 K and 873 K, ensuring cyclic thermoplastic deformation of samples. Subfigure (b) displays the ZDM-200PU elasto-plastic bending test machine: it clamps a φ50 mm specimen with one end connected to a fixed head and the other mounted on a moving table that applies upward bending load Q, with a constant 150 mm free end deflection for cyclic elasto-plastic bending tests. These apparatuses support the evaluation of thermal fatigue and elasto-plastic cyclic loading performance of Fe-Mn-Al-C steels, verifying their superior fatigue resistance compared to reference martensitic Cr steels.

This figure presents S-N (stress vs. number of cycles) fatigue curves for Fe-Mn-Al-C austenitic steel under three test conditions. The x-axis denotes the number of cycles N in units of 10^6, and the y-axis denotes cyclic stress +/- σ in MPa. Curve 1 (diamond markers) corresponds to fatigue tests at 293 K, curve 2 (circle markers) corresponds to tests at 673 K, and curve 3 (triangle markers) corresponds to thermocycling tests alternating between 293 K and 673 K. All three curves show a decreasing trend of tolerable stress as cycle number increases. The 673 K test yields the highest fatigue strength across all cycle counts, while the 293 K test yields the lowest; the thermocycling curve falls between these two, indicating thermocycling fatigue performance is intermediate between constant low and high temperature conditions. The observed higher fatigue strength at elevated temperature is attributed to time-dependent deformation aging strengthening in microplastic deformation zones, supporting the conclusion that Fe-Mn-Al-C steels are suitable for service under cyclic loading combined with periodic temperature changes.

This set of 4 optical micrographs, all equipped with 30 μm scale bars, demonstrates the microstructural evolution of two steel grades before and after cyclic loading and thermal tests. The first subgraph presents the as-prepared austenitic Fe-Mn-Al-C steel with uniform polyhedral austenite grain structure. The second subgraph shows the same Fe-Mn-Al-C steel after tests, where high-temperature aging leads to obvious thickening of κ phase precipitates at grain boundaries as reported. The third subgraph displays the untested martensitic Cr-Ni steel with characteristic oriented martensitic structure. The fourth subgraph exhibits the tested martensitic Cr-Ni steel, in which the original solid solution decomposes into an equilibrium ferrite-carbide mixture, the initial martensite orientation disappears, and mechanical strength decreases accordingly. The comparison of microstructure evolution between the two steels verifies their different structural stability under cyclic service conditions, supporting the core conclusion that Fe-Mn-Al-C austenitic steels possess better cyclic loading and thermal fatigue performance than reference martensitic Cr steels.

This figure is a force-deflection hysteresis diagram for φ50 mm Fe-Mn-Al-C austenitic steel specimens subjected to elasto-plastic cyclic bending tests. The x-axis represents deflection (H, unit: mm, range from -200 to +200 mm), while the y-axis represents applied load (Q, unit: ×10³ N, range from -50×10³ N to +50×10³ N). Three curves are plotted, corresponding to the 1st loading cycle, 2nd cycle, and 12th (last) cycle of the test. The hysteresis loop shape shows minimal change across increasing cycle counts, indicating the work spent on elastoplastic deformation per cycle remains nearly constant during repeated loading. This stable cyclic behavior demonstrates that Fe-Mn-Al-C steel has excellent elasto-plastic fatigue resistance: unfailed specimens of this alloy can sustain 4 times more loading cycles than comparative Fe-13Cr-0.2C martensitic chromium steel under identical test conditions, supporting its suitability for components under cyclic loading service conditions.

This figure presents the true stress-true strain curve of Fe-Mn-Al-C austenitic steel obtained from tensile test at a strain rate of 1 mm/min, characterizing its strain hardening behavior. The x-axis denotes true strain ranging from 0 to 0.4, while the y-axis represents true stress in the unit of MPa. The curve shows a steep initial rise corresponding to elastic deformation, followed by continuous work hardening during plastic deformation: true stress increases from around 500 MPa at ~0.02 true strain to approximately 1430 MPa at ~0.35 true strain, with total recorded true strain exceeding 0.35. The prominent strain hardening capacity of the alloy originates from mechanical twinning in its austenitic structure during plastic deformation, where deformation twins act as obstacles to dislocation slip and further twinning, supporting the core conclusion that Fe-Mn-Al-C steel possesses excellent endurance under cyclic elasto-plastic deformation conditions.

This figure contains two transmission electron microscopy (TEM) micrographs characterizing dislocation structures of Fe-Mn-Al-C austenitic steel after cold working, with a 625 nm scale bar visible in each subfigure. Subfigure (a) presents the microstructure after 10% cold working, while subfigure (b) shows the microstructure after 20% cold working. Visual observations confirm that dislocation density increases significantly with higher cold working degree, and localized dislocation tangles form at intersections of slip systems as deformation rises. These microstructural features support the core conclusion that the increased dislocation density and localized hardened zones generated during each cyclic loading event give the Fe-Mn-Al-C alloy high endurance under elasto-plastic cyclic deformation, with stable absorbed energy per loading cycle, explaining its superior fatigue performance relative to reference martensitic Cr steels.
